enrichment.short_description: Decidim is a free, open-source platform for participatory democracy that lets governments and organizations run proposals, budgets, and voting online.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Decidim is a free, open-source platform for citizen participation, proposals, budgeting, and elections. Used by governments and institutions worldwide.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Decidim (Catalan for 'we decide') is a free and open-source digital platform for participatory democracy that enables governments, public institutions, universities, and civil-society organizations to run online and hybrid citizen-participation processes such as proposal collection, participatory budgeting, public deliberation, surveys, assemblies, and secure elections. The project originated with Decidim.Barcelona, launched by Barcelona City Council on 31 January 2016 as part of the council's 2016-2019 citizen-participation action plan. The very first version was built as a fork of Consul, the open-source platform created by Madrid City Council (decide.madrid.es), reflecting the inter-municipal collaboration between the Ahora Madrid and Barcelona en Comu governments that emerged from Spain's 2015 municipal elections and the broader 15-M ('Indignados') movement's push for open, publicly owned democratic infrastructure. In 2017 the Decidim codebase was rewritten from scratch as a modular Ruby on Rails framework and began to be adopted by other cities and institutions beyond Barcelona. In 2019, Barcelona City Council transferred control of the Decidim trademark and codebase to the newly formed Decidim Association, a nonprofit that now stewards the project on behalf of a global community. Development and governance happen in the open on Metadecidim, a meta-participation space where developers, designers, public officials, researchers, and citizens co-design the platform's roadmap; the source code is published on GitHub under the AGPL-3.0 license, guaranteeing that any hosted derivative of Decidim must also make its source available.

Architecturally, Decidim is not a single hosted product but a self-hostable framework: any organization can deploy its own instance on its own infrastructure (Docker images and Rails deployment guides are provided) or engage one of the many third-party agencies and public-sector IT providers in the Decidim ecosystem to host, customize, and support an installation. There is no vendor lock-in and no mandatory license fee, since the software itself is entirely free; costs, when they exist, come from hosting, integration, and support services rather than the platform itself. Organizations configure 'participatory spaces' -- processes, assemblies, consultations, conferences, and initiatives -- and enrich each space with reusable components: Proposals (crowdsourced idea submission, amendment, and voting), Budgets (participatory budgeting where citizens allocate real public funds across competing projects), Meetings (in-person and online event scheduling with minutes and attendance), Surveys, Debates, Blogs, Pages, Accountability (tracking follow-through on commitments), and Elections (a secure, auditable digital voting booth supporting census upload or token-based voter verification). This component-based architecture lets a city run a multi-year strategic plan with public meetings and proposal collection in one space, while running a fast, single-issue participatory budget vote in another, all on the same underlying platform and user accounts.

Decidim's most prominent deployments include Barcelona itself, which has run several cycles of city-wide participatory budgeting through the platform, as well as instances used by the European Commission, the government of Mexico City, Helsinki, and dozens of other municipalities and regional governments across Europe and Latin America, along with universities and NGOs. Because it is free, source-available under a strong copyleft license, and backed by an active nonprofit and community rather than a single commercial vendor, Decidim is frequently chosen by public administrations that are required or prefer to use open, auditable civic-tech infrastructure rather than proprietary SaaS. Its closest competitors are Consul (the Madrid-originated open-source platform it originally forked from), CitizenLab/Go Vocal, Bang the Table/EngagementHQ, Balancing Act, and OpenGov's community engagement tools; Decidim differentiates itself primarily through its open governance model, AGPL licensing, breadth of built-in democratic mechanisms, and its academic/civic-tech lineage tied to Barcelona's technological-sovereignty movement.

Because Decidim is free software with no official pricing tiers, the cost of adoption is really the cost of hosting and configuration: small deployments can run on a modest VPS, while large national or EU-wide consultations require more substantial infrastructure and DevOps support, typically procured through the Decidim Association's directory of certified service providers. The platform's interface has been localized into dozens of languages via community translation on Crowdin, including English, Spanish, Catalan, French, German, Italian, Portuguese, Finnish, and Polish, making it viable for multilingual, multi-jurisdiction deployments. Target users are municipal and regional governments, national ministries, supranational bodies like the EU, universities, and any civil-society organization that needs an auditable, standards-based way to run public consultations, participatory budgets, or elections without depending on a single commercial vendor's roadmap or terms of service. Because the codebase is open and self-hosted, Decidim also appeals to public bodies with strict data-sovereignty or procurement requirements that rule out closed SaaS competitors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Decidim is a free and open-source digital platform for participatory democracy, letting governments, universities, and civil-society organizations run structured citizen-participation processes such as proposal collection, budgeting, and voting.

First launched by Barcelona City Council in January 2016 and rewritten as a modular Ruby on Rails framework in 2017, Decidim is now governed by the nonprofit Decidim Association and developed openly by a global community on the Metadecidim platform.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Decidim's participatory spaces (processes, assemblies, consultations, and initiatives) can be enriched with components including Proposals, Participatory Budgeting, Meetings, Surveys, Debates, Blogs, Accountability, and secure digital Elections.

Because it is self-hosted and released under the AGPL-3.0 license, any organization can deploy, customize, and extend Decidim on its own infrastructure without license fees or vendor lock-in, while remaining obligated to share modifications under the same open license.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Decidim itself is entirely free software with no licensing cost; organizations pay only for hosting and, optionally, implementation and support services from third-party agencies within the Decidim ecosystem.
This model appeals particularly to public-sector bodies with data-sovereignty or procurement requirements that favor open, auditable civic-tech infrastructure over proprietary SaaS subscriptions.

features.title: Proposals
features.description: Crowdsourced idea submission, amendment, and voting on citizen proposals.

features.title: Participatory Budgeting
features.description: Citizens allocate real public funds by voting on competing projects.

features.title: Secure Elections
features.description: Digital voting booth with census upload or token-based voter verification.

features.title: Meetings
features.description: Schedule and document in-person and online public meetings with minutes.

features.title: Assemblies & Processes
features.description: Structured, multi-phase participatory spaces for long-running initiatives.

features.title: Surveys & Debates
features.description: Structured feedback collection and open public discussion tools.

features.title: Accountability
features.description: Track government follow-through on commitments made during participation.

features.title: Self-Hosted & Open Source
features.description: AGPL-3.0 Ruby on Rails framework deployable on your own infrastructure with multi-language support.

pros: Completely free and open source under AGPL-3.0, no license fees
Broad set of built-in democratic tools, including binding elections and participatory budgeting
Backed by an active nonprofit and global open-governance community
Full data ownership and control via self-hosting
cons: Requires Ruby on Rails DevOps expertise to self-host and maintain
No official managed/hosted SaaS tier from the Decidim Association itself
Steeper learning curve for administrators than closed-source SaaS competitors
Design and UX customization requires development resources

faqs.question: Is Decidim free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Decidim is entirely free, open-source software; organizations only pay for hosting and optional support.

faqs.question: Who created Decidim?
faqs.answer: Barcelona City Council launched the first Decidim instance in January 2016; it is now governed by the nonprofit Decidim Association.

faqs.question: What license is Decidim released under?
faqs.answer: Decidim's source code is released under the AGPL-3.0 license.

faqs.question: Can I self-host Decidim?
faqs.answer: Yes, Decidim is designed to be self-hosted on your own infrastructure using Docker and Ruby on Rails deployment guides.

faqs.question: What is Decidim used for?
faqs.answer: Participatory budgeting, proposal collection, public deliberation, surveys, assemblies, and secure elections.

faqs.question: Who maintains Decidim today?
faqs.answer: The Decidim Association, a nonprofit, alongside a global community of contributors on Metadecidim.

use_cases.description: Institutions gather structured feedback and run internal democratic processes.

enrichment.short_description: Deel is a global payroll, HR, and Employer of Record platform that helps companies hire, pay, and manage employees and contractors in 150+ countries.
enrichment.headquarters: San Francisco, California, USA (fully distributed/remote team)
enrichment.seo_title: Deel: Global Payroll, EOR & HR Platform | 150+ Countries
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Deel is a global hiring platform offering EOR, contractor management, payroll, and HR tools in 150+ countries. Backed by $979M+ in funding, valued at $17.3B.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Deel is a global people-management platform that combines Employer of Record (EOR) services, contractor payments, global payroll, HR information systems (HRIS), immigration support, and equity management into a single system so that companies can legally hire, pay, and manage employees and independent contractors in more than 150 countries without setting up their own local legal entities. The company was founded in 2019 by Alex Bouaziz (CEO) together with co-founder Shuo Wang, initially focused on solving cross-border contractor payments before rapidly expanding into full Employer of Record services, which let a client company employ a worker in a country where the client has no legal entity by having Deel's local entity serve as the legal employer of record while the client directs the day-to-day work. Deel is headquartered in San Francisco but operates as a fully distributed, remote-first company, a structure the founders have described as both a philosophical choice and a practical necessity for a company selling global employment infrastructure.

Deel has raised roughly $979 million across eight funding rounds from investors including Ribbit Capital, Andreessen Horowitz (a16z), Coatue Management, Y Combinator, and Spark Capital, among others. Its most recent disclosed raise was a $300 million Series E co-led by Ribbit Capital, a16z, and Coatue, which valued the company at $17.3 billion, making Deel one of the most highly valued private HR-tech companies globally. As of 2026 the company reports roughly $1.4 billion in annual recurring revenue (up from around $1 billion the prior year), processes about $22 billion in payroll annually, and serves more than 35,000 customers and 1.5 million-plus workers across 150-plus countries. In February 2026, Deel filed a confidential S-1 with regulators, signaling active preparation for a public listing, though as of this writing it remains a private company. Headcount estimates vary significantly by data source and time period, ranging from roughly 4,000 to over 9,600 employees, reflecting Deel's rapid and sometimes volatile hiring trajectory.

Functionally, Deel's platform is organized around several core products that can be purchased independently or combined. Deel EOR lets a client hire a full-time employee in a country without a local entity; Deel's local subsidiary becomes the legal employer, issuing compliant employment contracts, running local payroll and tax withholding, administering statutory benefits, and handling onboarding/offboarding, typically priced around $599 per employee per month for standard countries and roughly $899 per employee per month for higher-complexity 'enterprise' EOR countries. Deel Contractor Management, aimed at companies paying independent contractors rather than employees, starts at roughly $49 per contractor per month and covers compliant contract generation, multi-currency payouts in 120-plus currencies, and tax form generation (such as 1099s or W-8BENs). For situations where contractor misclassification risk is a concern, Deel offers Contractor of Record (COR) at a higher price point (around $325/month) that converts the arrangement into a compliant, Deel-managed relationship. Deel Global Payroll, for companies that already have their own legal entities in a country and just need payroll processing, is priced from around $29 per employee per month. A US-specific Professional Employer Organization (PEO) offering is also available for domestic US employees, priced around $125 per employee per month. Across all of these products, customers get access to a unified HRIS dashboard covering time-off tracking, expense management, org charts, and reporting, plus 24/7 in-app support. One notable operational detail buyers frequently flag is that Deel typically requires a one-month gross-salary deposit per EOR employee, refunded roughly 30 days after offboarding, and applies a currency-conversion markup (commonly cited around 0.6% to 2% above the mid-market rate) when funding and payroll currencies differ.

Deel's target customers span the full range from early-stage startups making their first international hire to large enterprises running distributed teams of hundreds or thousands of workers; its scale-based volume discounts mean effective per-employee EOR pricing for large enterprise commitments (50-plus employees, multi-year terms) can be reported as low as roughly $315 to $400 per employee per month. Deel differentiates itself from competitors primarily on breadth (payroll, EOR, contractor management, immigration, and equity in one platform), country coverage (150-plus), and speed of onboarding, and has increasingly layered AI-assisted compliance and HR features on top of the core product. Its most direct competitors in the global-employment-platform category include Remote, Rippling, Papaya Global, Multiplier, Oyster HR, and Globalization Partners (G-P), with Rippling in particular being a frequent point of public rivalry given overlapping HRIS ambitions. Deel is available as a web application with companion iOS and Android mobile apps for employees and admins to manage time off, expenses, and payslips, and exposes APIs for HRIS and payroll integrations with tools like NetSuite, QuickBooks, and various applicant tracking platforms.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Deel is a global hiring and payroll platform that lets companies employ workers in 150+ countries through Employer of Record services, manage international contractors, and run global payroll and HR from one system.

Founded in 2019 by Alex Bouaziz, Deel has raised roughly $979 million and reached a $17.3 billion valuation following its Series E, reporting around $1.4 billion in annual recurring revenue and 35,000+ customers as of 2026.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Core products include Employer of Record (EOR), Contractor Management, Contractor of Record, Global Payroll, an HRIS dashboard, immigration support, and equity management, each usable independently or combined.
Deel supports payouts in 120+ currencies, offers 24/7 in-app support, and provides iOS and Android apps so employees and administrators can manage time off, expenses, and payslips from anywhere.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Pricing is per-employee or per-contractor and varies by product: Global Payroll starts around $29/month, Contractor Management around $49/month, Contractor of Record around $325/month, and EOR from roughly $599 to $899/month depending on country complexity.

Large enterprise commitments can negotiate lower effective EOR rates, but buyers should budget for a one-month gross-salary deposit per EOR employee and a currency-conversion markup on cross-currency payments.

features.title: Employer of Record (EOR)
features.description: Hire full-time employees abroad without setting up a local entity.

features.title: Contractor Management
features.description: Compliant contracts and multi-currency payouts in 120+ currencies.

features.title: Global Payroll
features.description: Run compliant payroll for your own owned entities in 100+ countries.

features.title: HRIS Dashboard
features.description: Unified view of time off, expenses, org charts, and reporting.

features.title: Immigration & Visa Support
features.description: Sponsor and manage work visas and employee relocation.

features.title: Equity Management
features.description: Issue and manage global equity grants for distributed teams.

features.title: Contractor of Record (COR)
features.description: Reduce contractor misclassification risk with a Deel-managed relationship.

features.title: Mobile Apps
features.description: iOS and Android apps for managing payroll, time off, and payslips on the go.

pros: Single platform covering EOR, payroll, contractor payments, and HR in 150+ countries
Fast onboarding for international hires, often within days
Backed by deep funding ($979M+) and a strong investor base signaling stability
Broad currency and payment-method support for global contractor payouts
cons: EOR and PEO pricing is significantly higher than pure payroll-only plans
Requires a one-month gross salary deposit per EOR employee, tying up cash
Currency conversion markup (0.6-2%) adds cost on cross-currency payments
Headcount and reported financials vary across sources, making external verification harder

faqs.question: What is Deel?
faqs.answer: A global platform for Employer of Record, contractor management, payroll, and HR across 150+ countries.

faqs.question: How much does Deel cost?
faqs.answer: Pricing is per-employee or per-contractor, starting around $29/month for payroll and reaching $599-$899/month for EOR.

faqs.question: What is Employer of Record (EOR)?
faqs.answer: A service where Deel's local entity legally employs a worker on a client's behalf in a country where the client has no entity.

faqs.question: Does Deel replace a legal entity?
faqs.answer: For EOR customers, yes; for Global Payroll customers, Deel processes payroll for entities the customer already owns.

faqs.question: Is Deel publicly traded?
faqs.answer: Not yet; Deel filed a confidential S-1 in February 2026 but remains a private company as of this writing.

faqs.question: What countries does Deel operate in?
faqs.answer: Deel supports hiring and payments in more than 150 countries.

use_cases.description: Send compliant, multi-currency payments to contractors worldwide.

use_cases.title: Consolidating payroll across owned entities
use_cases.description: Run payroll for existing international subsidiaries from one dashboard.

features: Local legal employment in higher-complexity jurisdictions

enrichment.short_description: DeepSeek is a Chinese AI lab that builds open-weight large language models like DeepSeek-V3 and R1, offered via a free chat app and a low-cost API.
enrichment.seo_title: DeepSeek: Open-Weight AI Models & Chat App | V3, R1
enrichment.seo_meta_description: DeepSeek is a Chinese AI lab offering free chat apps and low-cost API access to open-weight models DeepSeek-V3 and R1, rivaling top Western LLMs.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
DeepSeek is a Chinese artificial intelligence company that designs, trains, and releases large language models, most notably the general-purpose DeepSeek-V3 family and the reasoning-focused DeepSeek-R1 model, both of which are released as open-weight models that developers can download, run locally, and fine-tune. The company was founded in Hangzhou, Zhejiang, in July 2023 by Liang Wenfeng, a Chinese entrepreneur who is also co-founder of the quantitative hedge fund High-Flyer, which owns and funds DeepSeek. As of public disclosures, Liang personally holds a large majority stake in DeepSeek (reported at roughly 84% via two holding entities), and the company has largely self-funded its research using capital and compute resources built up through High-Flyer's algorithmic trading business rather than raising traditional venture capital, a structure that distinguishes it from most Western frontier AI labs such as OpenAI, Anthropic, and Google DeepMind.

DeepSeek rose to global prominence in January 2025 when it released DeepSeek-R1, a reasoning model using chain-of-thought techniques that benchmarked competitively against OpenAI's o1 model on math, coding, and logic tasks, while being released with open weights and at a fraction of the training and inference cost publicly attributed to comparable Western models. This triggered widespread commentary about the efficiency of DeepSeek's training methods (including techniques like mixture-of-experts architectures and reinforcement-learning-based post-training) and briefly moved global technology markets as investors reassessed assumptions about the capital intensity of frontier AI development. DeepSeek has continued to iterate on its model lineup, including DeepSeek-V3 (a general-purpose model with a large context window, roughly 164K tokens, strong at coding, reasoning, and multilingual tasks) and subsequent versions such as DeepSeek-V4, with the lab positioning itself around a strategy of matching or approaching frontier-model quality at substantially lower training and serving cost.

Access to DeepSeek's models comes in two main forms. The DeepSeek chat product is a free consumer-facing AI assistant, available via web browser at chat.deepseek.com as well as official native apps for iOS and Android, that lets anyone converse with the models, use a Deep Think reasoning mode, upload files, search the web, and sync chat history across devices; the official app is free, though DeepSeek has also warned that many unofficial 'DeepSeek' branded apps in mobile app stores are unaffiliated wrappers that may charge for functionality the real app provides at no cost. Developers and businesses can instead call DeepSeek's models programmatically through its API, priced per million tokens on a pay-as-you-go basis; as of 2026, DeepSeek-V3 is priced at roughly $0.269 per million input tokens and $0.40 per million output tokens, while DeepSeek-R1 (whose chain-of-thought reasoning generates substantially more output tokens per response) is priced at roughly $0.80 per million tokens for both input and output. DeepSeek-V4 pricing has been reported around $0.30 per million input tokens and $0.50 per million output tokens. The API also offers a context-caching discount, billing cache hits at roughly 10% of the standard input rate, which materially lowers cost for applications with repeated or templated prompts.

DeepSeek's models support well over 50 languages, with particularly strong performance in English and Mandarin Chinese and solid coverage of major European and Asian languages, making the platform attractive for multilingual applications. The company's primary differentiator versus competitors like OpenAI, Anthropic, Google, and Meta is the combination of open-weight licensing (which lets enterprises self-host models for data-sovereignty or cost reasons), aggressive low pricing on hosted API access, and research transparency around training methodology, all delivered from a lab that, unlike most large AI labs, is not backed by major outside venture or hyperscaler investment but rather by a proprietary trading firm's balance sheet. This has made DeepSeek's models popular building blocks for third-party inference providers, open-source tooling, and cost-sensitive enterprise deployments, while also drawing scrutiny in some Western markets over data handling, since DeepSeek's hosted consumer services route data through infrastructure based in China, leading several governments and enterprises to restrict or ban official DeepSeek apps on official devices even as the open-weight models themselves remain widely used when self-hosted elsewhere. Target users span individual developers experimenting with a free, capable assistant; startups and enterprises building cost-sensitive AI features on the API or self-hosted weights; and researchers studying efficient large-model training techniques. Competitors include OpenAI (ChatGPT/GPT models), Anthropic (Claude), Google (Gemini), Meta (Llama, also open-weight), Mistral, xAI's Grok, and other Chinese labs such as Alibaba's Qwen and Moonshot AI's Kimi.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: DeepSeek is a Chinese AI lab, founded in July 2023 by Liang Wenfeng and funded by the hedge fund High-Flyer, that develops open-weight large language models including DeepSeek-V3 and the reasoning-focused DeepSeek-R1.

DeepSeek rose to global attention in January 2025 when R1 matched leading Western reasoning models like OpenAI's o1 on math, coding, and logic benchmarks at a fraction of the reported training and serving cost.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Models are released with open weights, letting developers self-host and fine-tune them, while a free consumer chat app (web, iOS, Android) offers a Deep Think reasoning mode, web search, and file upload.

The API is priced per token, with DeepSeek-V3 around $0.27/$0.40 per million input/output tokens and R1 around $0.80 per million tokens, plus a caching discount that bills repeated-prompt hits at roughly 10% of the standard input rate.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The DeepSeek chat app is entirely free to use, while developers pay per token through the API on a pay-as-you-go basis with no subscription commitment.

DeepSeek's API pricing is consistently cited as significantly below comparable OpenAI and Anthropic models, which is central to its positioning as a low-cost, open-weight alternative to closed frontier labs.

features.title: DeepSeek-V3
features.description: General-purpose LLM with a large (~164K token) context window.

features.title: DeepSeek-R1
features.description: Chain-of-thought reasoning model for math, code, and logic tasks.

features.title: Free Chat App
features.description: Web, iOS, and Android app with a Deep Think reasoning mode.

features.title: Low-Cost API
features.description: Pay-per-token pricing with a 10% cache-hit discount on repeated prompts.

features.title: Open-Weight Releases
features.description: Models are downloadable and self-hostable rather than fully closed.

features.title: Web Search Integration
features.description: Built-in web search inside the chat interface.

features.title: File Upload & History Sync
features.description: Upload documents and sync multi-turn chat history across devices.

features.title: Multilingual Support
features.description: Strong performance across 50+ languages, especially English and Mandarin.

pros: Extremely competitive API pricing versus Western frontier labs
Open-weight models allow self-hosting and fine-tuning
Free consumer chat app with a dedicated reasoning mode
Strong benchmark performance on coding and math relative to cost
cons: Data handling and China-based infrastructure raise governance/privacy concerns for some enterprises and governments
Some governments and organizations restrict or ban the official app on official devices
Less mature ecosystem of official enterprise support compared to OpenAI, Anthropic, or Google
Unofficial third-party 'DeepSeek' apps in app stores can mislead users into paying for features the real app offers free

faqs.question: Is DeepSeek free?
faqs.answer: The chat app is free; the API is billed per token on a pay-as-you-go basis.

faqs.question: Who owns DeepSeek?
faqs.answer: DeepSeek is owned and funded by the hedge fund High-Flyer and was founded by Liang Wenfeng.

faqs.question: Are DeepSeek models open source?
faqs.answer: DeepSeek releases its models as open weights that can be downloaded and self-hosted.

faqs.question: What is DeepSeek-R1 used for?
faqs.answer: Chain-of-thought reasoning tasks such as math problems, code debugging, and multi-step planning.

faqs.question: Is DeepSeek safe to use?
faqs.answer: Consumer data is routed through China-based infrastructure, which has led some governments to restrict the official app; self-hosted open-weight deployments avoid that concern.

faqs.question: How does DeepSeek pricing compare to OpenAI?
faqs.answer: DeepSeek's per-token API pricing is significantly cheaper than comparable OpenAI and Anthropic models.

use_cases.title: Cost-sensitive AI application development
use_cases.description: Build LLM-powered features at a fraction of the token cost of leading closed models.

use_cases.title: Self-hosted enterprise LLM deployment
use_cases.description: Download open weights and run models on private infrastructure for data control.

use_cases.title: General AI assistant
use_cases.description: Use the free chat app for research, writing, and coding help.

pricing_plans.price: $0.27 per 1M input tokens / $0.40 per 1M output tokens

enrichment.short_description: DefectDojo is an open-source DevSecOps and ASPM platform that aggregates, deduplicates, and manages vulnerabilities from 200+ security scanning tools.
enrichment.pricing_model: Free/Open Source with paid Pro tier
enrichment.seo_meta_description: DefectDojo is an OWASP flagship open-source platform for vulnerability management, DevSecOps automation, and ASPM, with a Pro tier from $300/month.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
DefectDojo is an open-source vulnerability management and Application Security Posture Management (ASPM) platform used by security and DevSecOps teams to aggregate, correlate, deduplicate, triage, and report on findings from a wide range of security testing tools. The project traces its origins to 2013 and was released as open-source software in 2015; it is written in Python using the Django web framework and has been developed in close partnership with the OWASP Foundation, under which it holds status as an OWASP Flagship Project -- a designation reserved for mature, widely adopted projects that demonstrate strategic value to application security. The community/open-source edition of DefectDojo is distributed under the permissive BSD 3-Clause license, and the project reports substantial real-world adoption, with figures cited around 45 million-plus downloads, roughly 4,800 GitHub stars, close to 490 contributors, and more than 10,000 organizations using the platform.

At its core, DefectDojo is designed to solve a problem common to any organization running multiple security scanners: each tool (SAST, DAST, SCA, container scanning, infrastructure scanning, penetration testing reports, and more) produces its own report format and its own view of 'a vulnerability,' and without a central system the same underlying flaw gets reported, tracked, and remediated multiple times across different tools and teams. DefectDojo solves this by ingesting structured results from 200-plus supported security tools (including popular options like OWASP ZAP, Burp Suite, Checkmarx, Semgrep, Trivy, and Nessus, plus many CI/CD-native scanners), normalizing them into a common data model organized around products, engagements, tests, and findings, then applying deduplication logic so that the same vulnerability discovered by multiple tools (or the same tool run repeatedly) is merged into a single trackable finding rather than duplicated. From there, teams can triage findings, assign severity and risk ratings, track remediation status and SLAs, generate compliance-oriented reports, and expose metrics on security posture trends over time, functionally turning a pile of raw scanner output into an organized, auditable vulnerability management workflow that integrates into CI/CD pipelines via its REST API.

Alongside the free, self-hosted Community Edition, DefectDojo also offers a commercial Pro edition, sold both as a cloud-hosted SaaS product and as an on-premises installation, currently offered as a subscription starting at roughly $300 per month (with promotional discounts around 30% observed at various points). DefectDojo Pro adds capabilities not present in the community edition: enhanced dashboards and executive-level reporting, expanded risk-scoring and prioritization logic, a tunable deduplication engine that lets administrators fine-tune how findings are merged, a customizable rules engine for automating triage decisions, out-of-the-box connector integrations with enterprise-grade scanners such as Checkmarx One and Burp Suite Enterprise, and -- reflecting the broader industry shift toward AI-assisted security operations -- a feature called DefectDojo Sensei, an AI module aimed at helping teams interpret and act on findings faster, as well as Model Context Protocol (MCP) support that lets large language model assistants query and act on DefectDojo data directly. DefectDojo offers prospective Pro customers a two-week trial to evaluate the paid tier before committing.

DefectDojo's primary users are application security engineers, DevSecOps teams, and security leadership at organizations of any size that run more than one scanning tool and need a single source of truth for vulnerability status, a category that spans startups adopting their first SAST/DAST pipeline up to large enterprises consolidating security tooling across hundreds of development teams. Because the community edition is free, fully self-hostable, and integrates broadly, DefectDojo is frequently adopted by security teams that want to avoid vendor lock-in or per-seat SaaS pricing common among commercial ASPM vendors. Its competitors span both the open-source and commercial ends of the market: open-source alternatives and adjacent projects include OWASP Dependency-Track (software-composition/SBOM-focused rather than general vulnerability aggregation), while commercial ASPM and vulnerability-management competitors include products like Faraday, Vulcan Cyber, Kenna Security (Cisco), Brinqa, and larger platform players such as Snyk and Wiz that bundle scanning and vulnerability management together. DefectDojo differentiates itself primarily on tool-agnostic breadth (200-plus integrations), a mature and long-running open-source core that avoids lock-in, and a clear free-to-paid upgrade path (Community to Pro) for teams that later need enterprise scanner connectors, AI-assisted triage, or vendor support. DefectDojo deployments are typically run via Docker Compose, Kubernetes/Helm charts, or a hosted Pro instance, and the platform exposes a full REST API and CLI tooling so that findings ingestion and reporting can be automated as a stage inside CI/CD pipelines (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, Jenkins, and others), making it as much a piece of security-automation infrastructure as a standalone dashboard.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: DefectDojo is an open-source vulnerability management and ASPM platform that aggregates, deduplicates, and tracks findings from 200+ security testing tools, maintained as an OWASP Flagship Project since its 2013-2015 origins.
Written in Python/Django and licensed under BSD 3-Clause, DefectDojo reports adoption by more than 10,000 organizations and over 45 million downloads of its Community Edition.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform normalizes scanner output into products, engagements, tests, and findings, applies deduplication logic across tools and scan runs, and tracks remediation SLAs and compliance reporting.

A commercial Pro tier, available as cloud-hosted SaaS or on-premises from around $300/month, adds the AI-assisted DefectDojo Sensei module, Model Context Protocol support, a tunable deduplication rules engine, and enterprise scanner connectors.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Community Edition is completely free and self-hosted, making DefectDojo accessible to teams of any size without per-seat licensing costs.
Organizations that need AI-assisted triage, enterprise scanner integrations, or managed hosting can upgrade to DefectDojo Pro, which is also offered with a two-week trial.

UI can feel dense for teams new to vulnerability management workflows
Deduplication tuning may require manual configuration to avoid noise

faqs.answer: Yes, the Community Edition is free and open source under the BSD 3-Clause license.

faqs.answer: More than 200 security scanning and testing tools.

faqs.answer: Yes, DefectDojo is an OWASP Flagship Project.

faqs.answer: Yes, a two-week trial of DefectDojo Pro is offered.

faqs.answer: The Community Edition is released under the BSD 3-Clause license.

enrichment.short_description: DelightChat is an omnichannel helpdesk and WhatsApp marketing platform that helps D2C and e-commerce brands manage WhatsApp, Instagram, and email support.
enrichment.headquarters: Delaware, USA (legal); distributed team across India and Southeast Asia
enrichment.seo_title: DelightChat: WhatsApp Helpdesk & Marketing for E-commerce
enrichment.seo_meta_description: DelightChat unifies WhatsApp, Instagram, and email support with WhatsApp marketing automation for D2C brands. Plans start at $29/month with a 14-day trial.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
DelightChat is a customer support and WhatsApp marketing platform built specifically for direct-to-consumer (D2C) and e-commerce small and medium-sized businesses, unifying high-volume conversational channels -- the official WhatsApp Business API, Instagram Direct Messages, Facebook Messenger -- with traditional support channels like email and website live chat into a single shared team inbox. The company was founded in 2020 by Preetam Nath and Sankalp Jonna, who built the product around a specific pain point they observed among e-commerce brands: as WhatsApp and Instagram became primary customer-contact channels alongside traditional email, support teams were forced to juggle multiple disconnected apps and lost visibility into a shopper's full conversation history across channels. DelightChat's team is small, reported at roughly 13 to 16 employees, distributed primarily across India and Southeast Asia, with the company's registered legal headquarters in Delaware, United States.

The product is delivered both as a standalone web application and as a native Shopify app (listed on the Shopify App Store as 'DelightChat: WhatsApp Marketing + Helpdesk'), reflecting its tight focus on the Shopify-centric D2C ecosystem, though it also serves merchants on other e-commerce stacks. Functionally, DelightChat splits into two connected halves. The Helpdesk half centralizes customer conversations from WhatsApp, Instagram, Facebook, email, and live chat into one inbox with shared team visibility, assignment rules, canned responses, and order-context lookups (pulling a customer's Shopify order history directly into the conversation view so agents don't have to switch tabs to answer 'where is my order' questions). The Marketing half uses the official WhatsApp Business API to send bulk broadcast campaigns, abandoned-cart recovery messages, order and shipping notification flows, and other automated WhatsApp sequences, positioning WhatsApp as both a support and a revenue-recovery channel rather than purely a customer-service tool. An AI chatbot capability is also part of the newer product surface, aimed at automating first-response handling across Instagram, Facebook, email, and WhatsApp before escalating to a human agent.

DelightChat's pricing is structured in tiers: publicly listed plans include a Startup tier around $29 per month, a Scale tier around $99 per month, and a Growth tier around $299 per month, with feature and usage limits (number of WhatsApp conversations, team seats, automation rules) scaling up at each tier; the company also offers a 14-day free trial, after which several sources report an effective starting price closer to $49 per month depending on the specific plan and promotional terms active at signup. As a young, independent, VC-light company relative to category leaders, DelightChat has reported modest but real revenue traction, with third-party estimates citing roughly $1.8 million in annual recurring revenue and a company valuation in the single-digit millions, positioning it as a lean, profitable-leaning niche player rather than a venture-scale unicorn.

DelightChat's target customer is squarely the growing D2C or e-commerce SME that has outgrown generic helpdesk tools like Zendesk or Freshdesk for WhatsApp-specific workflows, or has outgrown using the WhatsApp Business App manually and needs the official WhatsApp Business API with multi-agent support and marketing automation. Its most direct competitors include Wati, Interakt, Gallabox, MyAlice, and Zoko -- all WhatsApp Business API-focused helpdesk/marketing tools serving a similar SME e-commerce audience, especially strong in markets like India and Southeast Asia where WhatsApp commerce is particularly dominant -- as well as broader omnichannel helpdesk platforms like Gorgias and Richpanel that serve e-commerce brands but with less WhatsApp-specific depth. DelightChat differentiates itself through its combination of helpdesk and WhatsApp marketing automation in one product, tight Shopify integration showing order data inline with conversations, and pricing aimed at SMEs rather than enterprise budgets. The product is delivered entirely as a web application (with a companion Shopify app listing) rather than native desktop or mobile apps, and support is available through email, live chat, WhatsApp, and a self-serve help center. Because DelightChat is API-based, using Meta's official WhatsApp Business Platform rather than an unofficial or scraped WhatsApp integration, messaging is compliant with WhatsApp's commerce policies and templated-message approval process, which is a meaningful trust signal for merchants wary of account bans that can occur with unofficial WhatsApp automation tools.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: DelightChat is an omnichannel helpdesk and WhatsApp marketing platform built for D2C and e-commerce SMEs, founded in 2020 by Preetam Nath and Sankalp Jonna.
It unifies the WhatsApp Business API, Instagram, Facebook, email, and live chat into one team inbox, with tight Shopify integration that surfaces order data directly inside conversations.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The Helpdesk half centralizes multi-channel conversations with assignment rules and canned responses, while the Marketing half uses the official WhatsApp Business API for broadcast campaigns, abandoned-cart recovery, and shipping notifications.
An AI chatbot handles first-response automation across channels before escalating to human agents, and the product is available as a web app plus a native Shopify App Store listing.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Plans are tiered at roughly $29 (Startup), $99 (Scale), and $299 (Growth) per month, with a 14-day free trial available before committing to a paid plan.
Pricing scales with conversation volume, team seats, and automation depth, positioning DelightChat as an affordable alternative to enterprise helpdesk suites for growing e-commerce brands.

faqs.answer: Plans start around $29-49/month with a 14-day free trial.

faqs.answer: Yes, it uses Meta's official WhatsApp Business API.

faqs.question: Is DelightChat only for Shopify?
faqs.answer: It has a native Shopify app but also supports other channels and store platforms.

faqs.answer: Preetam Nath and Sankalp Jonna founded DelightChat in 2020.

faqs.answer: Yes, alongside WhatsApp, email, and live chat, all in one inbox.

enrichment.short_description: Deluge is a free, open-source, cross-platform BitTorrent client with a client/daemon architecture supporting web, console, and desktop interfaces.
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Deluge is a free, open-source, cross-platform BitTorrent client with a lightweight daemon, web UI, and plugin system. Available for Windows, Mac, and Linux.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Deluge is a free and open-source, cross-platform BitTorrent client written primarily in Python, released under the GPL-3.0-or-later license. The project's first version was released on September 25, 2006; it was originally going to be named something else but was renamed 'Deluge' because a project called gtorrent already existed on SourceForge, and because the developers had by then extended their ambitions beyond a GNOME-only client to something that could run on any platform supporting GTK. Deluge is a community/volunteer open-source project rather than a commercial company, hosted and developed collaboratively on GitHub under the deluge-torrent organization, with no venture funding, corporate ownership, or paid tiers -- the entire application, including all official plugins, is free to download, use, and modify.

Architecturally, Deluge is built around libtorrent, a widely used C++ networking library that implements the actual BitTorrent protocol logic (peer discovery, piece selection, encryption, and transfer), which Deluge wraps via its own Python bindings. Starting with version 1.0, Deluge adopted a client/daemon split: the core torrent-handling engine (deluged) runs as a background daemon/service, independent of any particular user interface, which can then be controlled remotely by one or more separate front-ends -- a native GTK desktop GUI, a text-based console interface (useful for headless servers and SSH sessions), and a browser-based Web UI that lets users manage torrents remotely from any device with a browser. This separation is one of Deluge's most distinctive architectural traits relative to many other BitTorrent clients, since it allows a user to run the daemon persistently on a home server, NAS, or seedbox and then connect to it from a desktop GUI, phone browser, or terminal without the interface needing to run on the same machine as the actual downloading process. Deluge has supported magnet links since version 1.1.0 (released January 2009) and, per the 0.5.x rewrite, added protocol encryption, peer exchange (PEX), and UPnP support; it also implements DHT (Distributed Hash Table) for trackerless torrent discovery, Local Peer Discovery, NAT-PMP for automatic port forwarding, and Web Seeds (HTTP-based torrent seeding).

A large portion of Deluge's advanced functionality is delivered through an official plugin system rather than being bundled into the core application, keeping the base client lightweight while still letting power users add capabilities such as RSS-based auto-downloading, bandwidth scheduling, notifications, label-based torrent organization, and various third-party-contributed extensions. Deluge runs natively on Linux (its primary and most actively maintained platform, commonly available through distribution package repositories), and has official or community-supported builds for Windows and macOS (historically distributed via MacPorts, with more recent native builds also available), making it genuinely cross-platform despite its GTK/GNOME origins. Because the daemon-and-remote-UI architecture is particularly well suited to always-on, headless environments, Deluge is a popular choice for users running dedicated download boxes, NAS devices (including via community packages for Synology and QNAP systems), and remote seedboxes, where the Web UI or console interface lets them manage transfers without needing a full desktop environment.

As free, open-source software, Deluge's target users are individuals and technically inclined users who want a lightweight, scriptable, remotely manageable torrent client without subscription costs or bundled adware -- a category of user who values control and self-hosting over the more polished, sometimes ad-supported or freemium experience offered by some competing clients. Its most direct competitors in the open-source BitTorrent client space are qBittorrent (widely seen today as Deluge's closest and most actively developed rival, with a similarly ad-free, open-source Qt-based interface and its own built-in search and RSS features), Transmission (particularly popular on macOS and Linux for its minimalism), and, among closed-source/freemium options, uTorrent and BitTorrent (both owned by Rainberry, Inc.), which bundle advertising and, historically, extra features gated behind a paid 'Pro' tier that Deluge and its open-source peers do not have. Deluge's translated interface is maintained by a volunteer community on Launchpad and covers dozens of languages beyond English, including German, French, Spanish, Italian, Russian, Chinese, and Japanese, among others, reflecting its long-running global open-source contributor base. Deluge is fully free with no pricing tiers, no ads, and no telemetry-driven monetization, consistent with its GPL-3.0-or-later licensing, which requires that any distributed modified version of the software also remain open source.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Deluge is a free, open-source, cross-platform BitTorrent client first released in 2006 and licensed under GPL-3.0-or-later, developed by a volunteer community rather than a company.

Built on the libtorrent networking library, Deluge splits its core torrent engine into a background daemon that can be controlled remotely by a GTK desktop GUI, a text console, or a browser-based Web UI.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Deluge supports magnet links, DHT, peer exchange, protocol encryption, UPnP, NAT-PMP, and web seeds, with additional capabilities like RSS auto-downloading and bandwidth scheduling available through its official plugin system.
Its client/daemon architecture makes it especially well suited to headless servers, NAS devices, and seedboxes, where users manage transfers remotely without running a full desktop environment.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Deluge is entirely free with no paid tiers, ads, or telemetry-driven monetization, consistent with its GPL open-source licensing.
Its closest free competitor is qBittorrent, while paid or ad-supported clients like uTorrent gate certain features behind subscriptions that Deluge does not require.

Actively translated into dozens of languages by a volunteer community
cons: Interface feels dated compared to some modern competitors like qBittorrent
Development pace has historically been slower and less consistent than top rivals
Best experience is on Linux; Windows and macOS builds have lagged at times

faqs.question: Is Deluge free?
faqs.answer: Yes, Deluge is fully free and open source under GPL-3.0-or-later.

faqs.question: What platforms does Deluge support?
faqs.answer: Linux, Windows, and macOS.

faqs.question: Can I run Deluge on a headless server?
faqs.answer: Yes, via the daemon combined with the Web UI or console interface.

faqs.question: Does Deluge support magnet links?
faqs.answer: Yes, since version 1.1.0 released in January 2009.

faqs.question: What underlying library does Deluge use?
faqs.answer: libtorrent, a C++ BitTorrent networking library.

faqs.question: How is Deluge different from qBittorrent?
faqs.answer: Both are free and open source; Deluge's key distinction is its dedicated daemon/remote-UI architecture.

enrichment.short_description: Demio is a browser-based webinar platform for marketers, offering live, automated, and evergreen webinars with built-in engagement tools and analytics.
enrichment.seo_title: Demio: Browser-Based Webinar Software for Marketers
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Demio is a no-download webinar platform offering live, automated, and evergreen webinars with engagement tools. Plans start around $45/month, 14-day trial.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Demio is a browser-based webinar hosting platform built specifically for marketing teams that want to run live, automated, and evergreen ('recurring/on-demand') webinars without asking attendees to download any software. The product was launched in 2014-2015 by co-founders Wyatt Jozwowski and David Abrams, who built Demio to address what they identified as a persistent problem in the webinar-software market at the time: existing platforms were clunky, required desktop downloads or plugins, and created technical friction for both hosts and attendees that hurt registration-to-attendance conversion rates for marketing webinars. Demio operated as an independent, standalone SaaS company through the 2010s before being acquired in early 2021 by Banzai International, Inc., a Seattle-area marketing-technology holding company; Demio is now one of several branded products under the Banzai umbrella, alongside other demand-generation and event-marketing tools, and Banzai itself is a publicly traded company (listed as BNZI).

The core product design philosophy behind Demio is a fully in-browser experience -- hosts create a webinar 'room,' configure registration pages and reminder emails, and both hosts and attendees join the live session directly through a web browser with no app installation, a design choice aimed squarely at maximizing attendee show-up rates by removing friction at the moment someone clicks the join link. Within the room, Demio provides interactive engagement tools including live polls, a chat panel, a dedicated Q&A queue, downloadable handouts, audience reactions, and a feature called 'Featured Actions,' which lets hosts surface a call-to-action, offer, or resource directly inside the webinar player at a chosen moment (for example, pushing a special pricing link partway through a product demo webinar). Beyond live sessions, Demio supports Automated and Evergreen webinars -- pre-recorded sessions that play back on a schedule or on-demand, simulating a live experience (including simulated live chat) so that marketing teams can keep a high-performing webinar generating leads long after the original live delivery, a category of feature commonly associated with evergreen webinar funnel marketing tactics. Demio also provides registration-source tracking, integrations with common marketing and CRM tools (for pushing registrant and attendee data into email and marketing-automation platforms), and post-webinar analytics covering attendance, engagement, and drop-off.

Demio's pricing is structured in tiers gated by both room capacity (maximum simultaneous attendees) and feature access. The entry-level Starter plan is aimed at teams running occasional webinars for smaller audiences (commonly cited around a 50-attendee room with one host) with pricing beginning around $45 per month when billed annually. The Growth plan, generally cited starting around $75 per host per month (billed annually) for a larger room size (around 150 attendees), is positioned for companies running webinars more frequently as a core part of their marketing motion, and is the tier at which several of Demio's most requested capabilities unlock -- notably Automated/Evergreen webinars, registration-source-level analytics, and deeper custom branding controls, meaning teams that need automation specifically should budget for Growth rather than Starter. Larger attendee-capacity and multi-host requirements push customers into higher, more custom-priced tiers. A 14-day free trial is available across plans, letting prospective customers test the live-webinar experience before committing to a paid tier.

Demio's target users are marketing teams -- demand generation, product marketing, and customer-education functions -- at B2B SaaS and services companies that run webinars as a recurring lead-generation and nurture channel, rather than large broadcast-scale corporate town halls or massive public conferences, which tend to be better served by platforms designed for much higher attendee ceilings. Its most direct competitors in the marketing-webinar category include Zoom Webinars/Zoom Events, WebinarJam and its evergreen-focused sibling EverWebinar, GoTo Webinar, Livestorm, and BigMarker, with EverWebinar in particular being a frequent direct comparison point given its dedicated focus on evergreen/automated webinar funnels. Demio differentiates itself primarily on its no-download, fully browser-based attendee experience, its polished built-in engagement toolkit (Featured Actions, reactions, live Q&A), and its positioning as being purpose-built for marketers rather than IT/enterprise-communications teams, in contrast to broader video-conferencing platforms like Zoom that added webinar features on top of a general video-calling product. Since being folded into Banzai International's product suite, Demio has increasingly been sold and cross-promoted alongside Banzai's other demand-generation tools, reflecting a broader strategy of bundling webinar hosting with adjacent marketing-technology capabilities for B2B marketing teams.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Demio is a browser-based webinar platform for marketers, founded in 2014-2015 by Wyatt Jozwowski and David Abrams and acquired by Banzai International, Inc. in early 2021.
Its core design principle is a no-download, fully in-browser experience for both hosts and attendees, aimed at maximizing webinar show-up rates.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: Demio supports live, automated, and evergreen webinars, with built-in engagement tools including polls, chat, Q&A, reactions, handouts, and Featured Actions for in-webinar calls-to-action.
Registration-source analytics and marketing/CRM integrations help teams track which channels drive attendance, while custom branding controls unlock on higher-tier plans.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: The Starter plan begins around $45/month (billed annually) for a 50-attendee room with one host, while the Growth plan, from around $75/host/month, unlocks automated and evergreen webinars plus deeper analytics.
A 14-day free trial lets teams test the live-webinar experience before subscribing, and larger attendee capacities are available on higher, more custom-priced tiers.

enrichment.short_description: Dependency-Track is a free, open-source OWASP platform that uses SBOMs to continuously identify and manage risk across the software supply chain.
enrichment.seo_title: Dependency-Track: Open-Source SBOM & Supply Chain Security
enrichment.seo_meta_description: Dependency-Track is a free OWASP platform for SBOM analysis, tracking components, vulnerabilities, and policy across your software supply chain at scale.
enrichment.llm_readable_summary:
Dependency-Track is a free and open-source platform for software supply chain security, built around the concept of the Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) rather than traditional point-in-time dependency scans. The project began in 2013 under the OWASP Foundation umbrella (created by Steve Springett, who remains its lead architect) and has since become one of OWASP's Flagship Projects, a status reserved for projects that demonstrate strategic value and sustained maturity within the application security community. Dependency-Track is released under the Apache 2.0 license, making it freely usable, modifiable, and embeddable -- including in commercial products -- without royalty obligations, and the project reports adoption by more than 20,000 organizations worldwide.

The platform's central architectural idea is to treat the SBOM as the unit of truth for a software project's composition. Rather than scanning source code or a build artifact fresh each time a report is needed, Dependency-Track ingests CycloneDX-format SBOMs (an OWASP-originated, now internationally standardized SBOM format) generated by build tooling, CI/CD pipelines, or dedicated SBOM-generation tools, and continuously tracks every component -- libraries, containers, operating system packages, firmware, and services -- across every version of every project in an organization's portfolio. Because the SBOM captures a project's full dependency tree at a point in time, Dependency-Track can retroactively surface risk the moment a new vulnerability is disclosed for any component already recorded in a previously ingested SBOM, without needing to re-scan the original codebase; this 'ingest once, monitor continuously' model is what most clearly distinguishes SBOM-based platforms like Dependency-Track from traditional Software Composition Analysis (SCA) tools that primarily scan on demand. Dependency-Track continuously cross-references components against multiple vulnerability intelligence sources, verifies upstream component integrity, and prioritizes remediation using signals like EPSS (Exploit Prediction Scoring System) scores to help teams focus on vulnerabilities that are both severe and realistically exploitable rather than triaging every CVE with equal urgency. The platform also produces and consumes CycloneDX VEX (Vulnerability Exploitability eXchange) and VDR (Vulnerability Disclosure Report) documents, letting organizations formally communicate whether a given vulnerability in a component is actually exploitable in their specific product context -- an increasingly important capability for organizations subject to supply-chain security regulation and customer due-diligence questionnaires.

Dependency-Track underwent a significant architectural overhaul with its version 5.x release line, which rebuilt the core engine for higher availability and much larger scale. The redesigned engine supports stateless application instances that coordinate purely through PostgreSQL (without requiring a separate message broker), enabling active/active high-availability deployments across multiple zones. Early adopters running v5 alpha and beta builds reportedly ingested upwards of 20,000 SBOMs per hour, and some organizations have been reported running single v5 instances holding more than 250,000 SBOMs representing tens of millions of individual software components -- throughput and portfolio scale that puts Dependency-Track firmly in enterprise-grade territory despite it being entirely free, open-source software. As with most OWASP projects, Dependency-Track has no official commercial SaaS offering or paid tier from the OWASP Foundation itself; organizations self-host it (commonly via Docker containers, with deployment guides for Kubernetes and cloud environments) and, where commercial support or managed hosting is desired, turn to third-party vendors and consultancies in the OWASP ecosystem rather than a single official vendor.

Dependency-Track's primary users are application security and DevSecOps teams, particularly at organizations with large, multi-project software portfolios who need centralized, continuous visibility into open-source and third-party component risk -- a need sharpened in recent years by supply-chain-security regulation and frameworks (such as US federal software supply chain requirements around SBOMs for government software vendors) that increasingly make SBOM production and monitoring a compliance requirement rather than a best practice. It integrates naturally into CI/CD pipelines via its REST API, accepting SBOMs generated by tools like CycloneDX's own generators, Syft, or various language-specific SBOM plugins, and is frequently deployed alongside complementary OWASP tooling such as DefectDojo (which focuses on broader vulnerability management and finding deduplication across many types of security tools, including SBOM-based results) and OWASP Dependency-Check (a traditional, on-demand SCA scanner that Dependency-Track can also ingest results from). Its competitors in the broader software composition analysis and SBOM space include commercial platforms such as Snyk, Sonatype (Nexus Lifecycle/IQ), JFrog Xray, Mend (formerly WhiteSource), and Anchore, most of which bundle SBOM/SCA capability into broader paid application-security suites -- Dependency-Track differentiates itself by being purpose-built around SBOM ingestion specifically, free and open source with no per-seat or per-scan pricing, and backed by OWASP's nonprofit, vendor-neutral governance rather than a single commercial vendor's roadmap.

long_form_blocks.heading: Overview
paragraphs: Dependency-Track is a free, open-source OWASP Flagship Project for software supply chain security, originating in 2013 and licensed under Apache 2.0.

It centers on the Software Bill of Materials (SBOM) as its unit of truth, ingesting CycloneDX SBOMs to continuously track components across every version of every project in an organization's portfolio.

long_form_blocks.heading: Key Features
paragraphs: The platform continuously cross-references components against multiple vulnerability sources, prioritizes remediation using EPSS exploitability scores, and produces/consumes CycloneDX VEX and VDR documents.

Its redesigned v5 engine runs stateless instances coordinating through PostgreSQL alone, enabling active/active high-availability deployments that have been reported handling 250,000+ SBOMs and tens of millions of components in production.

long_form_blocks.heading: Pricing
paragraphs: Dependency-Track is completely free and self-hosted, with no official commercial SaaS tier or licensing cost from the OWASP Foundation.
Organizations needing managed hosting or commercial support typically work with third-party vendors in the OWASP ecosystem rather than a single official vendor.

features.title: SBOM Ingestion (CycloneDX)
features.description: Continuous component tracking across every version of every project.

features.title: Continuous Vulnerability Monitoring
features.description: Retroactive alerts the moment a new CVE affects a tracked component.

features.title: EPSS-Based Prioritization
features.description: Focus remediation effort on realistically exploitable risk.

features.title: VEX/VDR Support
features.description: Communicate real-world exploitability of component vulnerabilities.

features.title: Policy Engine
features.description: Enforce license and component policies across the portfolio.

features.title: High-Availability v5 Architecture
features.description: Stateless, active/active scaling coordinated through PostgreSQL.

features.title: REST API & CI/CD Integration
features.description: Automate SBOM ingestion as a pipeline stage.

features.title: Multi-Project Portfolio View
features.description: Track components across every version of every project in one place.

pros: Completely free and open source under the permissive Apache 2.0 license
Purpose-built for continuous, SBOM-based monitoring rather than one-off scans
Backed by OWASP's vendor-neutral, nonprofit governance
Proven to scale to enterprise-size portfolios (250,000+ SBOMs in production reports)
cons: Requires self-hosting and infrastructure management, with no official managed SaaS
Relies on quality, complete SBOMs from upstream build tooling to be effective
No official commercial support channel from OWASP itself
Steeper setup and learning curve than turnkey commercial SCA products

faqs.question: Is Dependency-Track free?
faqs.answer: Yes, it is free and open source under the Apache 2.0 license.

faqs.question: What is an SBOM?
faqs.answer: A Software Bill of Materials, a structured list of all components in a software project.

faqs.question: Who maintains Dependency-Track?
faqs.answer: The OWASP Foundation and its open-source contributor community.

faqs.question: What SBOM format does Dependency-Track use?
faqs.answer: CycloneDX, an OWASP-originated, internationally standardized SBOM format.

faqs.question: Can Dependency-Track integrate with CI/CD?
faqs.answer: Yes, via its REST API for automated SBOM ingestion.

faqs.question: How is Dependency-Track different from DefectDojo?
faqs.answer: Dependency-Track focuses specifically on SBOM-based component and vulnerability tracking, while DefectDojo aggregates findings across many types of security tools.

use_cases.title: Continuous open-source component risk monitoring
use_cases.description: Track vulnerabilities across every project and dependency version automatically.

use_cases.title: Meeting SBOM compliance requirements
use_cases.description: Produce and manage SBOMs required by regulatory or procurement mandates.

use_cases.title: Portfolio-wide supply chain risk visibility
use_cases.description: Give security leadership a single view of component risk across the organization.
